clusters.name: Maintenance Inspection Report
clusters.description: Periodic A/B/C/D-check inspection findings with ATA-chapter references, corrective actions, and DGCA-licensed sign-off.

clusters.name: Flight Test Report
clusters.description: Certification and acceptance flight test runs per CAR-23 / CS-25, with test conditions, parameters measured, and crew sign-off.

clusters.name: Service Bulletin
clusters.description: Manufacturer-issued modification advisories with ATA chapter, effectivity range, compliance window, and approved engineering authority.

fields.name: inspection_date
fields.description: Calendar date the inspection was performed

fields.name: total_flight_hours
fields.description: Total airframe flight hours at inspection

fields.name: total_flight_cycles
fields.description: Total airframe flight cycles at inspection

fields.name: findings
fields.description: List of findings with corrective actions

fields.name: next_inspection_due
fields.description: Date the next inspection is due

fields.name: narrative_summary
fields.description: One-line summary of the inspector's narrative paragraph (derived from prose)
fields.example: Pressurization controller anomaly logged for trend monitoring

fields.name: investigation_reference
fields.description: Investigation / surveillance reference cited in narrative

fields.name: defect_severity
fields.description: Qualitative severity inferred from prose (minor / moderate / significant)

fields.name: recommendation_text
fields.description: Inspector's free-form recommendation extracted from narrative
fields.example: Recommend component replacement at the next available C-check, not to exceed 1,500 FH.

groups.name: Inspection Scope
groups.description: Check level, ATA chapter, AMM reference

groups.name: Certification
groups.description: Inspector sign-off and next due date

groups.name: Narrative Findings
groups.description: Fields derived from the prose narrative section

fields.name: test_objective
fields.description: Stated objective per the test plan

fields.name: observations
fields.description: Test observations bulleted by the FTE

fields.name: conclusion
fields.description: Pass / pass with observations / partial pass

fields.name: discussion_summary
fields.description: Concise summary of the prose discussion paragraph
fields.example: Handling satisfactory; minor lateral PIO suppressed by reduced stick input

fields.name: handling_qualities_rating
fields.description: Qualitative HQR derived from discussion prose

fields.name: anomaly_described
fields.description: Anomaly or surprise mentioned in pilot prose (free-form)
fields.example: Brief 0.3 g vertical disturbance at flaps transition

fields.name: certification_recommendation
fields.description: Recommendation extracted from the discussion section
fields.example: Recommend approval of the test point for certification basis.

groups.name: Pilot Discussion
groups.description: Fields derived from the prose discussion section

fields.name: reason
fields.description: Why the SB is being issued

fields.name: background_summary
fields.description: Summary of the background/rationale narrative paragraph
fields.example: Field experience identified accelerated bearing wear under cold-soak conditions

fields.name: in_service_incident_count
fields.description: Number of in-service occurrences cited in prose (extracted as integer)

fields.name: risk_classification
fields.description: Risk category inferred from narrative (LOW / MINOR / MAJOR / HAZARDOUS)

fields.name: residual_risk_text
fields.description: Residual risk statement extracted from narrative

groups.name: Risk Narrative
groups.description: Fields derived from the prose background section

doc_snippets:
MAINTENANCE INSPECTION REPORT

Work Order Number:   MIR/2026/23434
Date of Inspection:  08/02/2026
Station:             Delhi (DEL) — Hangar Bay 3

AIRCRAFT DETAILS

Aircraft Type:       Embraer E190
Registration:        VT-JCD
Operator:            Star Air
Total Flight Hours:  17,144
Total Flight Cycles: 15,066
Engine Type:         CF34-10E

INSPECTION

Check Level:         A-Check
ATA Chapter:         21 — Air Conditioning
Reference Manual:    AMM Rev. 46, Task 21-88-13

FINDINGS AND CORRECTIVE ACTIONS

  1. Cabin pressurization outflow valve actuator response time 1.2s, within limit.
     ACTION: Component replaced per IPC reference; functional test PASSED.
  2. Forward galley chiller coolant level below MIN mark, replenished.
     ACTION: Borescope inspection scheduled for next A-Check.
  3. FADEC software version mismatch found, software update SB-FADEC-22 applied.
     ACTION: Adjusted to manufacturer specification; recorded in tech log.
  4. Leading edge slat actuator showing minor hydraulic seepage within AMM limits.
     ACTION: Lubrication performed per MM 12-21-04; signed off.

INSPECTOR'S NARRATIVE

An anomaly was noted in the cabin pressurization controller during the post-flight test, with the outflow valve commanding fully open for approximately 2 seconds during the automatic schedule transition. Subsequent ground tests were unable to reproduce the condition, and BITE data showed no fault codes. The event has been logged under investigation reference INV-21-2026-0418 for continued trend monitoring.

Recommend component replacement at the next available C-check, not to exceed 1,500 FH.

CERTIFICATION

Aircraft is hereby certified airworthy for return to service in accordance with the
approved maintenance program. All applicable Airworthiness Directives reviewed and
found compliant.

Inspected by:        Arjun Kapoor, B2
DGCA Licence No.:    AME-95181
Signature:           _____________________   Date: 08/02/2026

Next Inspection Due: 28/06/2026
MAINTENANCE INSPECTION REPORT

Work Order Number:   MIR/2026/65392
Date of Inspection:  26/01/2026
Station:             Delhi (DEL) — Hangar Bay 3

AIRCRAFT DETAILS

Aircraft Type:       Boeing 737 MAX 8
Registration:        VT-AKO
Operator:            Akasa Air
Total Flight Hours:  18,463
Total Flight Cycles: 14,438
Engine Type:         CFM LEAP-1B27

INSPECTION

Check Level:         B-Check
ATA Chapter:         36 — Pneumatic
Reference Manual:    AMM Rev. 44, Task 36-23-55

FINDINGS AND CORRECTIVE ACTIONS

  1. Lavatory waste tank flush motor intermittent; cleaned and tested OK.
     ACTION: Deferred to next scheduled inspection per MEL Cat-B (10 days).
  2. L1 wheel brake assembly worn to 28% remaining; replacement scheduled.
     ACTION: Component replaced per IPC reference; functional test PASSED.
  3. APU oil consumption rate elevated to 0.18 L/hr; trend monitoring continued.
     ACTION: Lubrication performed per MM 12-21-04; signed off.

INSPECTOR'S NARRATIVE

The pilot's defect entry referenced a momentary EICAS message during cruise on flight VT-EXP-2024-118. The FDR data was reviewed; the message lasted 0.4 seconds and was consistent with a known transient on the bleed pressure regulator under low-power conditions at high altitude. The current population of this regulator is being tracked under engineering bulletin EB-36-014. No corrective action required at this inspection.

Recommend dispatch under MEL Cat-B (10 days) pending parts availability.

CERTIFICATION

Aircraft is hereby certified airworthy for return to service in accordance with the
approved maintenance program. All applicable Airworthiness Directives reviewed and
found compliant.

Inspected by:        Arjun Kapoor, B2
DGCA Licence No.:    AME-44671
Signature:           _____________________   Date: 26/01/2026

Next Inspection Due: 28/03/2026

MAINTENANCE INSPECTION REPORT

Work Order Number:   MIR/2026/16175
Date of Inspection:  17/04/2026
Station:             Delhi (DEL) — Hangar Bay 3

AIRCRAFT DETAILS

Aircraft Type:       Boeing 737 MAX 8
Registration:        VT-AKO
Operator:            Akasa Air
Total Flight Hours:  13,957
Total Flight Cycles: 15,382
Engine Type:         CFM LEAP-1B27

INSPECTION

Check Level:         C-Check
ATA Chapter:         28 — Fuel System
Reference Manual:    AMM Rev. 47, Task 28-19-59

FINDINGS AND CORRECTIVE ACTIONS

  1. L1 wheel brake assembly worn to 28% remaining; replacement scheduled.
     ACTION: Borescope inspection scheduled for next A-Check.
  2. Static port heater P/N 387-2241-04 failed BIT, replaced with new unit.
     ACTION: Cleared after re-inspection; no further action required.

INSPECTOR'S NARRATIVE

Routine inspection of the main landing gear assembly revealed light surface corrosion on the lower torque link, classified as Cat-1 per CMM 32-11-04. The affected area was cleaned, treated with CIC-2 corrosion inhibitor, and re-protected per the approved process. The condition does not affect airworthiness and continued operation is permitted with re-inspection at the next A-check.

Recommend dispatch under MEL Cat-B (10 days) pending parts availability.

CERTIFICATION

Aircraft is hereby certified airworthy for return to service in accordance with the
approved maintenance program. All applicable Airworthiness Directives reviewed and
found compliant.

Inspected by:        R. Venkatesan, AME L1
DGCA Licence No.:    AME-71348
Signature:           _____________________   Date: 17/04/2026

Next Inspection Due: 28/07/2026

MAINTENANCE INSPECTION REPORT

Work Order Number:   MIR/2026/52487
Date of Inspection:  12/02/2026
Station:             Delhi (DEL) — Hangar Bay 3

AIRCRAFT DETAILS

Aircraft Type:       Boeing 787-8
Registration:        VT-ANI
Operator:            Air India
Total Flight Hours:  8,037
Total Flight Cycles: 12,813
Engine Type:         GEnx-1B70

INSPECTION

Check Level:         Phase-2
ATA Chapter:         28 — Fuel System
Reference Manual:    AMM Rev. 41, Task 28-83-20

FINDINGS AND CORRECTIVE ACTIONS

  1. Leading edge slat actuator showing minor hydraulic seepage within AMM limits.
     ACTION: Deferred to next scheduled inspection per MEL Cat-B (10 days).
  2. Forward galley chiller coolant level below MIN mark, replenished.
     ACTION: Adjusted to manufacturer specification; recorded in tech log.
  3. Right main landing gear oleo strut servicing low pressure, recharged to spec.
     ACTION: Component replaced per IPC reference; functional test PASSED.

INSPECTOR'S NARRATIVE

During the scheduled inspection the crew reported a mild buffet on rotation, observed intermittently over the last three sectors. Initial walk-around revealed a small area of paint blistering on the left wing fairing, approximately 40 mm in diameter, with no underlying structural deformation evident. Tap-testing of the surrounding skin produced no anomalies, and dye-penetrant inspection of the adjacent fasteners returned negative. The aircraft was returned to service with continued monitoring; a follow-up borescope inspection is recommended at the next opportunity not later than 250 flight hours.

Recommend referral to engineering for trend analysis before next heavy maintenance visit.

CERTIFICATION

Aircraft is hereby certified airworthy for return to service in accordance with the
approved maintenance program. All applicable Airworthiness Directives reviewed and
found compliant.

Inspected by:        R. Venkatesan, AME L1
DGCA Licence No.:    AME-19071
Signature:           _____________________   Date: 12/02/2026

Next Inspection Due: 28/06/2026

FLIGHT TEST REPORT

Test Report Number:  FTR-BL-2026-294
Test Date:           26/04/2026
Test Duration:       2h 13min
Test Location:       Bengaluru (BLR) — Flight Test Range B

TEST AIRCRAFT

Aircraft Type:       De Havilland DHC-6
Registration:        VT-RDA
Engine Configuration: 2 × PT6A-34
Test Configuration:  89% MTOW, fwd CG @ 24% MAC

TEST CREW

Test Pilot:          Capt. V. Ramachandran
First Officer:       F/O R. Bhattacharya
Flight Test Engineer: FTE V. Subramanian

TEST OBJECTIVE

Wet Runway Stopping Distance — Verify anti-skid efficiency, stopping distance ratio for the subject aircraft at the conditions defined in
Test Plan FTR-BL-2026-294, in accordance with CAR-23 / CS-25 § 25.846.

TEST CONDITIONS

Weather:             CAVOK, wind 270/08, OAT 26°C
Test Altitude(s):    FL253 — FL135
Fuel On Board (Start): 9.3 tonnes
Aircraft Mass:       94 tonnes
CG Position:         24.8% MAC

OBSERVATIONS

  • Cabin pressure schedule tracked normally; max ΔP within limit.
  • Buffet onset noted at α = 9.4°, well clear of stall AoA (12.8°).
  • Landing gear retraction time 9.8s (spec: ≤ 12.0s); extension 11.4s.
  • Roll control authority adequate at all tested conditions; no adverse handling noted.

DISCUSSION

Overall the aircraft demonstrated handling qualities consistent with Level 1 per MIL-STD-1797A criteria across the tested envelope. One minor finding: the FBW law exhibited a brief 0.3 g vertical disturbance at the flaps transition point, which was traced to a known software characteristic scheduled for resolution in software build P5.2. No safety-of-flight concern; acceptable for continued certification testing.

Recommend re-flight at fwd CG / max weight before release.

CONCLUSION

PASS — all acceptance criteria met. Recommended for certification.

Approved by:         Capt. V. Ramachandran
FTE Concurrence:     FTE V. Subramanian
FLIGHT TEST REPORT

Test Report Number:  FTR-BL-2026-927
Test Date:           28/01/2026
Test Duration:       2h 41min
Test Location:       Bengaluru (BLR) — Flight Test Range B

TEST AIRCRAFT

Aircraft Type:       Boeing 737 MAX 8
Registration:        VT-AKO
Engine Configuration: 2 × CFM LEAP-1B27
Test Configuration:  75% MTOW, fwd CG @ 25% MAC

TEST CREW

Test Pilot:          Capt. A. Deshmukh
First Officer:       F/O R. Bhattacharya
Flight Test Engineer: FTE V. Subramanian

TEST OBJECTIVE

Single-Engine Out Handling — Verify Vmca, asymmetric thrust, rudder authority for the subject aircraft at the conditions defined in
Test Plan FTR-BL-2026-927, in accordance with CAR-23 / CS-25 § 25.319.

TEST CONDITIONS

Weather:             CAVOK, wind 270/08, OAT 26°C
Test Altitude(s):    FL285 — FL110
Fuel On Board (Start): 13.2 tonnes
Aircraft Mass:       142 tonnes
CG Position:         18.6% MAC

OBSERVATIONS

  • FADEC reported no exceedances; engine vibration < 0.4 IPS throughout.
  • Pitch attitude steady within ±0.5° of target throughout maneuver.
  • Engine parameters remained within nominal envelope during entire test sequence.
  • Cabin pressure schedule tracked normally; max ΔP within limit.

DISCUSSION

Engine response during the rapid-power-application sequence was within predicted timing, with spool-up from idle to TOGA achieved in 5.9 seconds (specification ≤ 8.0 s). No compressor stall or surge indications were observed at any combination of altitude and speed flown. Recommend release of the engine envelope expansion package for line operations pending completion of the final cold-weather test campaign.

Recommend incorporation into FCOM Vol. 2, Chapter 5 — Abnormal Procedures.

CONCLUSION

PASS — exceeds spec margin by 8%. No further action required.

Approved by:         Capt. A. Deshmukh
FTE Concurrence:     FTE V. Subramanian
FLIGHT TEST REPORT

Test Report Number:  FTR-BL-2026-258
Test Date:           22/06/2026
Test Duration:       3h 47min
Test Location:       Bengaluru (BLR) — Flight Test Range B

TEST AIRCRAFT

Aircraft Type:       Airbus A350-900
Registration:        VT-JRC
Engine Configuration: 2 × RR Trent XWB
Test Configuration:  80% MTOW, fwd CG @ 25% MAC

TEST CREW

Test Pilot:          Capt. M. Choudhary
First Officer:       F/O A. Krishnan
Flight Test Engineer: FTE K. Banerjee

TEST OBJECTIVE

Brake Energy Limit Test — Verify RTO at MTOW, brake temperatures, fuse plug release for the subject aircraft at the conditions defined in
Test Plan FTR-BL-2026-258, in accordance with CAR-23 / CS-25 § 25.615.

TEST CONDITIONS

Weather:             BKN at 8000ft, wind calm, OAT 14°C
Test Altitude(s):    FL351 — FL160
Fuel On Board (Start): 9.0 tonnes
Aircraft Mass:       175 tonnes
CG Position:         20.2% MAC

OBSERVATIONS

  • Roll control authority adequate at all tested conditions; no adverse handling noted.
  • Pitch attitude steady within ±0.5° of target throughout maneuver.
  • FADEC reported no exceedances; engine vibration < 0.4 IPS throughout.
  • Buffet onset noted at α = 9.4°, well clear of stall AoA (12.8°).

DISCUSSION

Aircraft handling characteristics throughout the test envelope were qualitatively assessed as satisfactory, with no compensatory pilot inputs required to maintain target attitudes. Initial pitch response was crisp and well-damped, with predicted overshoot of 1.8° decaying within a single cycle. Roll asymmetry of approximately 0.4° was noted at flaps-15 configuration, attributed to fuel imbalance present at the start of the maneuver sequence, and considered insignificant for certification purposes.

Recommend release to line operations with no further restriction.

CONCLUSION

PASS — all acceptance criteria met. Recommended for certification.

Approved by:         Capt. M. Choudhary
FTE Concurrence:     FTE K. Banerjee
FLIGHT TEST REPORT

Test Report Number:  FTR-HY-2026-140
Test Date:           08/05/2026
Test Duration:       2h 26min
Test Location:       Bengaluru (BLR) — Flight Test Range B

TEST AIRCRAFT

Aircraft Type:       Airbus A320neo
Registration:        VT-EXP
Engine Configuration: 2 × PW1127G-JM
Test Configuration:  73% MTOW, fwd CG @ 24% MAC

TEST CREW

TEST OBJECTIVE

Cruise Performance Validation — Verify TAS at FL370, SFC, range factor for the subject aircraft at the conditions defined in
Test Plan FTR-HY-2026-140, in accordance with CAR-23 / CS-25 § 25.235.

TEST CONDITIONS

Weather:             SCT at 12000ft, wind 180/15, OAT 22°C
Test Altitude(s):    FL410 — FL233
Fuel On Board (Start): 18.7 tonnes
Aircraft Mass:       125 tonnes
CG Position:         20.0% MAC

OBSERVATIONS

  • Landing gear retraction time 9.8s (spec: ≤ 12.0s); extension 11.4s.
  • FADEC reported no exceedances; engine vibration < 0.4 IPS throughout.
  • Buffet onset noted at α = 9.4°, well clear of stall AoA (12.8°).
  • Stick force gradient consistent with predicted simulator response.

DISCUSSION

Overall the aircraft demonstrated handling qualities consistent with Level 1 per MIL-STD-1797A criteria across the tested envelope. One minor finding: the FBW law exhibited a brief 0.3 g vertical disturbance at the flaps transition point, which was traced to a known software characteristic scheduled for resolution in software build P5.2. No safety-of-flight concern; acceptable for continued certification testing.

Recommend release to line operations with no further restriction.

CONCLUSION

PASS WITH OBSERVATIONS — see noted items; cleared subject to FCOM revision.

Approved by:         Capt. S. Mukherjee
FTE Concurrence:     FTE K. Banerjee
FLIGHT TEST REPORT

Test Report Number:  FTR-DA-2026-457
Test Date:           17/03/2026
Test Duration:       2h 52min
Test Location:       Bengaluru (BLR) — Flight Test Range B

TEST AIRCRAFT

Aircraft Type:       ATR 72-600
Registration:        VT-IRR
Engine Configuration: 2 × PW127M
Test Configuration:  94% MTOW, fwd CG @ 26% MAC

TEST CREW

Test Pilot:          Capt. R. Subramanian
First Officer:       F/O A. Krishnan
Flight Test Engineer: FTE V. Subramanian

TEST OBJECTIVE

Climb Performance Evaluation — Verify rate of climb, fuel burn, time to altitude for the subject aircraft at the conditions defined in
Test Plan FTR-DA-2026-457, in accordance with CAR-23 / CS-25 § 25.407.

TEST CONDITIONS

Weather:             Few CU at 4000ft, wind 090/12, OAT 18°C
Test Altitude(s):    FL133 — FL148
Fuel On Board (Start): 17.4 tonnes
Aircraft Mass:       74 tonnes
CG Position:         21.8% MAC

OBSERVATIONS

  • Buffet onset noted at α = 9.4°, well clear of stall AoA (12.8°).
  • EICAS/ECAM displays remained clean; no nuisance alerts during test.
  • Stick force gradient consistent with predicted simulator response.
  • Roll control authority adequate at all tested conditions; no adverse handling noted.

DISCUSSION

The crew noted a mild lateral PIO tendency during the first approach in the gust simulation leg, which was attributed to the unfamiliar augmented control law. After the second exposure the pilot was able to suppress the tendency through reduced stick input amplitude. Recommend a brief familiarization brief be added to the type-rating syllabus covering this specific control law mode.

Recommend continued investigation under engineering surveillance.

CONCLUSION

PASS — all acceptance criteria met. Recommended for certification.

Approved by:         Capt. R. Subramanian
FTE Concurrence:     FTE V. Subramanian

FLIGHT TEST REPORT

TEST AIRCRAFT

Aircraft Type:       Airbus A321neo
Registration:        VT-IUR
Engine Configuration: 2 × CFM LEAP-1A26
Test Configuration:  62% MTOW, fwd CG @ 25% MAC

TEST CREW

Test Pilot:          Capt. S. Mukherjee
First Officer:       F/O P. Iyengar
Flight Test Engineer: FTE V. Subramanian

TEST OBJECTIVE

Single-Engine Out Handling — Verify Vmca, asymmetric thrust, rudder authority for the subject aircraft at the conditions defined in
Test Plan FTR-BL-2026-921, in accordance with CAR-23 / CS-25 § 25.459.

TEST CONDITIONS

Weather:             BKN at 8000ft, wind calm, OAT 14°C
Test Altitude(s):    FL236 — FL196
Fuel On Board (Start): 23.3 tonnes
Aircraft Mass:       51 tonnes
CG Position:         24.6% MAC

OBSERVATIONS

  • Stick force gradient consistent with predicted simulator response.
  • Engine parameters remained within nominal envelope during entire test sequence.
  • Cabin pressure schedule tracked normally; max ΔP within limit.
  • Hydraulic systems Green and Yellow maintained nominal pressure (3000 psi ± 50).

DISCUSSION

Engine response during the rapid-power-application sequence was within predicted timing, with spool-up from idle to TOGA achieved in 5.9 seconds (specification ≤ 8.0 s). No compressor stall or surge indications were observed at any combination of altitude and speed flown. Recommend release of the engine envelope expansion package for line operations pending completion of the final cold-weather test campaign.

Recommend continued investigation under engineering surveillance.

CONCLUSION

PASS WITH OBSERVATIONS — see noted items; cleared subject to FCOM revision.

Approved by:         Capt. S. Mukherjee
FTE Concurrence:     FTE V. Subramanian

SERVICE BULLETIN

Service Bulletin No: SB-BOE-72-866
Issue Date:          13/06/2026
Revision Status:     Original Issue
ATA Chapter:         72 — Engine
Classification:      Recommended

SUBJECT

Engine Fuel Pump Inspection

APPLICABILITY

Aircraft Type:       Boeing 737 MAX 8
Serial Numbers:      MSN 2664 through MSN 3275
Engine(s):           CFM LEAP-1B27
Effectivity:         All aircraft within the serial range listed above unless previously
                     modified per SB-BOE-72-411 Rev. 02.

REASON FOR ISSUE

This Service Bulletin is issued to address potential premature wear of impeller bearing under specific operating conditions. Operator reports and in-service
investigations indicate that continued operation without the corrective action described
herein may result in degraded system performance and increased maintenance burden.

BACKGROUND AND RISK ASSESSMENT

This Service Bulletin is issued in response to a small number of operator-reported events involving intermittent system status messages on the affected ATA chapter. Root cause analysis attributed the events to a latent software state that can occur when the system is energized within a specific 4-second window following the loss-of-bus event. The probability of encountering this state is assessed as remote, but the manufacturer has elected to release a software update to eliminate the failure mode.

Residual risk after compliance: NEGLIGIBLE.

DESCRIPTION OF CHANGE

Inspect impeller bearing per Figure 2 using borescope. If wear exceeds 0.15 mm, replace per AMM 73-12-04.

COMPLIANCE

Recommended compliance: Within 6,000 flight hours
or 18 months from the date of this Service Bulletin,
whichever occurs first.

ESTIMATED LABOR

Manhours:            11 MH per aircraft (two technicians, station-level)
Estimated Material Cost: USD 2,701
Special Tooling:     None / Standard line tooling

REFERENCES

— Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) Chapter 72
— Illustrated Parts Catalog (IPC) Chapter 72
— Component Maintenance Manual 72-69-66

APPROVED BY

Dr. K. Raghavan, Lead Engineer
Engineering Authority: EA-8244
Type Certification:    TC-FAA-2017
SERVICE BULLETIN

Service Bulletin No: SB-BOE-36-273
Issue Date:          26/06/2026
Revision Status:     Revision 02
ATA Chapter:         36 — Pneumatic
Classification:      Mandatory

SUBJECT

Hydraulic Reservoir Fill Valve Replacement

APPLICABILITY

Aircraft Type:       Boeing 787-8
Serial Numbers:      MSN 3112 through MSN 3841
Engine(s):           GEnx-1B70
Effectivity:         All aircraft within the serial range listed above unless previously
                     modified per SB-BOE-36-443 Rev. 02.

REASON FOR ISSUE

This Service Bulletin is issued to address corrosion of fill valve seat observed in field operations exceeding 8,000 FH. Operator reports and in-service
investigations indicate that continued operation without the corrective action described
herein may result in degraded system performance and increased maintenance burden.

BACKGROUND AND RISK ASSESSMENT

The original design assumption regarding the operating temperature range of this component has been challenged by field experience accumulated over the past 18 months. Investigation of returned units has identified that prolonged exposure to high-cycle vibration in combination with cold-soak conditions can lead to accelerated wear of the internal bearing surfaces. To date there have been 14 confirmed in-service occurrences across the global fleet, none of which have resulted in any safety-of-flight consequence. Engineering risk assessment categorizes the residual risk as Low after compliance with this Service Bulletin.

Residual risk after compliance: NEGLIGIBLE.

DESCRIPTION OF CHANGE

Replace fill valve assembly P/N HRV-2241-01 with improved variant P/N HRV-2241-02.

COMPLIANCE

Recommended compliance: Within 4,500 flight hours
or 12 months from the date of this Service Bulletin,
whichever occurs first.

ESTIMATED LABOR

Manhours:            11 MH per aircraft (two technicians, station-level)
Estimated Material Cost: USD 527
Special Tooling:     None / Standard line tooling

REFERENCES

— Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) Chapter 36
— Illustrated Parts Catalog (IPC) Chapter 36
— Component Maintenance Manual 36-35-28

APPROVED BY

T. Balakrishnan, Test Engineer
Engineering Authority: EA-1400
Type Certification:    TC-DGCA-India-2017
SERVICE BULLETIN

Service Bulletin No: SB-BOE-71-566
Issue Date:          28/01/2026
Revision Status:     Revision 01
ATA Chapter:         71 — Power Plant
Classification:      Recommended

SUBJECT

Wing Leading Edge Slat Track Lubrication Procedure Update

APPLICABILITY

Aircraft Type:       Boeing 777-300ER
Serial Numbers:      MSN 3943 through MSN 4706
Engine(s):           GE90-115BL
Effectivity:         All aircraft within the serial range listed above unless previously
                     modified per SB-BOE-71-606 Rev. 02.

REASON FOR ISSUE

This Service Bulletin is issued to address intermittent slat actuator stall reports traced to insufficient lubrication. Operator reports and in-service
investigations indicate that continued operation without the corrective action described
herein may result in degraded system performance and increased maintenance burden.

BACKGROUND AND RISK ASSESSMENT

Following the certification flight test campaign for the most recent engine variant, an additional thermal margin requirement was identified in the bleed air duct manifold downstream of the precooler. This Service Bulletin describes the inspection procedure and provides part number information for the strengthened replacement assembly. Operators with affected MSN are advised to schedule compliance at the next available C-check to minimize operational disruption.

Residual risk after compliance: NEGLIGIBLE.

DESCRIPTION OF CHANGE

Apply MIL-PRF-81322 grease per revised procedure detailed in Appendix A every 1500 FH.

COMPLIANCE

Recommended compliance: Within 3,000 flight hours
or 6 months from the date of this Service Bulletin,
whichever occurs first.

ESTIMATED LABOR

Manhours:            8 MH per aircraft (two technicians, station-level)
Estimated Material Cost: USD 3,809
Special Tooling:     None / Standard line tooling

REFERENCES

— Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) Chapter 71
— Illustrated Parts Catalog (IPC) Chapter 71
— Component Maintenance Manual 71-23-64

APPROVED BY

P. Krishnan, Design Engineer
Engineering Authority: EA-4585
Type Certification:    TC-DGCA-India-2025
SERVICE BULLETIN

Service Bulletin No: SB-BOE-36-355
Issue Date:          02/05/2026
Revision Status:     Original Issue
ATA Chapter:         36 — Pneumatic
Classification:      Recommended

SUBJECT

Cargo Door Latch Actuator Software Update

APPLICABILITY

Aircraft Type:       Boeing 787-8
Serial Numbers:      MSN 4283 through MSN 4808
Engine(s):           GEnx-1B70
Effectivity:         All aircraft within the serial range listed above unless previously
                     modified per SB-BOE-36-672 Rev. 02.

REASON FOR ISSUE

This Service Bulletin is issued to address rare nuisance unlock indication on EICAS during taxi. Operator reports and in-service
investigations indicate that continued operation without the corrective action described
herein may result in degraded system performance and increased maintenance burden.

BACKGROUND AND RISK ASSESSMENT

Continued surveillance of the in-service fleet has revealed that approximately 4% of the affected population exhibits a measurable performance shift in the subject system after 6,000 flight hours. The shift is gradual and does not produce any flight deck indication, but can lead to a small reduction in system efficiency. Compliance with this Service Bulletin will restore performance to within the original specification and is recommended at the next scheduled maintenance opportunity.

Risk classification per FHA: MINOR.

DESCRIPTION OF CHANGE

Upgrade actuator firmware from v3.4.1 to v3.5.0 using DCDS loader per maintenance task 52-71-01.

COMPLIANCE

Recommended compliance: Within 6,000 flight hours
or 24 months from the date of this Service Bulletin,
whichever occurs first.

ESTIMATED LABOR

Manhours:            6 MH per aircraft (two technicians, station-level)
Estimated Material Cost: USD 2,049
Special Tooling:     None / Standard line tooling

REFERENCES

— Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) Chapter 36
— Illustrated Parts Catalog (IPC) Chapter 36
— Component Maintenance Manual 36-80-67

APPROVED BY

T. Balakrishnan, Test Engineer
Engineering Authority: EA-3607
Type Certification:    TC-FAA-2021

Following the certification flight test campaign for the most recent engine variant, an additional thermal margin requirement was identified in the bleed air duct manifold downstream of the precooler. This Service Bulletin describes the inspection procedure and provides part number information for the strengthened replacement assembly. Operators with affected MSN are advised to schedule compliance at the next available C-check to minimize operational disruption.

Risk classification per FHA: MINOR.

DESCRIPTION OF CHANGE

Perform calibration per FCOM 28-41-00, comparing to drip-stick measurement at three fuel levels.

COMPLIANCE

Recommended compliance: Within 1,500 flight hours
or 24 months from the date of this Service Bulletin,
whichever occurs first.

ESTIMATED LABOR

Manhours:            12 MH per aircraft (two technicians, station-level)
Estimated Material Cost: USD 2,042
Special Tooling:     None / Standard line tooling

REFERENCES

— Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) Chapter 21
— Illustrated Parts Catalog (IPC) Chapter 21
— Component Maintenance Manual 21-12-79

APPROVED BY

A. Vasudevan, Senior Engineer
Engineering Authority: EA-1853
Type Certification:    TC-EASA-2017
SERVICE BULLETIN

Service Bulletin No: SB-EMB-24-141
Issue Date:          25/06/2026
Revision Status:     Original Issue
ATA Chapter:         24 — Electrical Power
Classification:      Mandatory

SUBJECT

Brake Control Unit Software Update

APPLICABILITY

Aircraft Type:       Embraer E190
Serial Numbers:      MSN 1817 through MSN 1887
Engine(s):           CF34-10E
Effectivity:         All aircraft within the serial range listed above unless previously
                     modified per SB-EMB-24-256 Rev. 02.

REASON FOR ISSUE

This Service Bulletin is issued to address FAA reported potential anti-skid logic discrepancy under wet runway conditions. Operator reports and in-service
investigations indicate that continued operation without the corrective action described
herein may result in degraded system performance and increased maintenance burden.

BACKGROUND AND RISK ASSESSMENT

A recent in-service occurrence on an operator in the Asia-Pacific region prompted a fleet-wide review of the component history. The investigation determined that aircraft operating in high-humidity, high-cycle environments are statistically more likely to exhibit the wear pattern described in section 3. While no immediate corrective action is mandated, the manufacturer strongly recommends compliance within the timeframe specified to maintain expected component reliability and dispatch availability.

Residual risk after compliance: NEGLIGIBLE.

DESCRIPTION OF CHANGE

Update BCU software from v2.18 to v2.20 per maintenance procedure 32-44-10.

COMPLIANCE

Recommended compliance: Within 6,000 flight hours
or 6 months from the date of this Service Bulletin,
whichever occurs first.

ESTIMATED LABOR

Manhours:            9 MH per aircraft (two technicians, station-level)
Estimated Material Cost: USD 4,069
Special Tooling:     None / Standard line tooling

REFERENCES

— Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) Chapter 24
— Illustrated Parts Catalog (IPC) Chapter 24
— Component Maintenance Manual 24-82-37

APPROVED BY

T. Balakrishnan, Test Engineer
Engineering Authority: EA-8619
Type Certification:    TC-FAA-2018
